text: The supplier should have submitted the ITP by last Friday.
There must have been a miscommunication about the hold points.
The waiver might have been approved by the wrong authority.
They could not have run the FAT. The cabinet was still in the factory.
Thanks for joining, Mei. Let's start with the open items. I'd like to follow up on the revised baseline schedule. It was due three weeks ago.
Yes, I know. The schedule is still with our engineering team in Shanghai.
I understand. But it should have reached us on June 10th, and we are now planning the site acceptance test without a date. There must have been a bottleneck on your side.
Possibly. My team might have been waiting for the interface document from your side.

To some extent I understand your position, however the interface document was signed in April. I have to push back on that. And I'd like to flag up a second issue: the inspection test plan you sent has no hold points.
That was intentional. We proposed witness points instead, to protect the production schedule.

That is the elephant in the room, and it should have been discussed with us before the ITP was submitted. So let's agree on a corrective action: you re-issue the ITP with the four hold points, and you send the schedule by Friday. Can we reach a consensus on that?
Friday is tight, but yes. I will send both by Friday, and I will copy my director, so that nobody is surprised later.
I'd like to follow up on the revised baseline schedule.
The inspection test plan should have been submitted by last Friday.
To some extent I understand your position, however I have to push back on that date.
Can we agree on a corrective action and a new deadline?

João, this is Mei Lin Zhao from CASCO. I owe you an update after our call on Monday. First, the revised baseline schedule. It left engineering yesterday, and you will have it before Friday, exactly as I promised. Second, the inspection test plan. You were right to push back on it. I looked at the original requirement and I found that the hold points were removed by our documentation team, not by engineering. There must have been a misunderstanding when the template was updated in May. We are re-issuing the plan with all four hold points restored, and the corrective action is registered in our system. Third, and I want to flag this up before you hear it somewhere else. The factory acceptance test may have to move by one week, because the interlocking cabinet arrived from our own supplier with a damaged connector. It is a small non-conformance, and honestly, we should have caught it at goods-in. I did not want you to find that in an email. Talk to you on Friday.

Good morning. Before you dial into that call, three things about escalation, because I think we get this wrong as an organization. First. Escalation is not a punishment. If a supplier misses a date twice, and you have followed up on it in writing twice, escalation is simply the next step in the process. In most of our packages it should have happened weeks earlier. Second. Never escalate a problem you have not flagged up first. If the first time your counterpart hears about a non-conformance is from his own director, you have not managed the issue. You have ambushed him, and he will never help you again. Third. Escalate the issue, not the person. The corrective action was not implemented on time. That is a fact, and it belongs in the minutes. Their engineer is incompetent. That is an opinion, and it does not. One more thing, and then I will let you go. When they ask for a waiver, ask yourself what that waiver actually costs us on site. A waiver that saves the supplier three days in the factory can cost us three weeks during the site acceptance test. That is not a good deal, and you should push back on it.
The revised baseline schedule should have reached us on June 10th.
There must have been a miscommunication about the hold points.
The drawings might have been sent to the wrong contact.
I would like to follow up on the revised baseline schedule.
I have to push back on that date, it is not realistic.
Can we agree on a corrective action and a new deadline?
I'd like to follow up on the revised baseline schedule.
The inspection test plan should have been submitted by last Friday.
To some extent I understand your position, however I have to push back on that date.
